## Definition

Mitochondria are double-membraned organelles containing both an outer *mitochondrial membrane* and *inner mitochondrial membrane*, with a small fluid filled space between them. The large, fluid-filled cavity enclosed by the inner membrane is known as the *mitochondrial matrix*.

## Core Principles

The inner membrane is folded into tubules called *cristae*, which substantially increase the surface area for the electron transport chain. Similar to the nucleus, mitochondria also contain their own special DNA (which we inherit from our mothers), which can make 13 proteins that are needed to make other important proteins for the mitochondria. As mitochondria can also make their own proteins, ribosomes (i.e. the protein-making factories) are present in the mitochondrial matrix.

## Sub-concepts

- Outer mitochondrial membrane
- Inner mitochondrial membrane
- Intermembrane space (small fluid-filled space between outer and inner membranes)
- Cristae (tubular folds of the inner membrane)
- Mitochondrial matrix (fluid-filled cavity enclosed by the inner membrane)
- Mitochondrial DNA (maternally inherited; encodes 13 proteins)
- Mitochondrial ribosomes (located in the matrix)

## Practical Applications

The expanded surface area provided by cristae supports the electron transport chain, underpinning mitochondrial ATP production relevant to cellular bioenergetics in sport and exercise contexts.

## Related Concepts

- Cytoskeleton (section 3.7.3.4)
- Nucleus (analogous DNA-containing organelle)
- Electron transport chain
- Ribosomes
